g.data-narration: Welcome to wait functions in UNIX process management. This lesson covers essential functions for parent-child process synchronization.
g.text: Wait Functions in UNIX/Linux Process Management

g.data-narration: When a child process terminates, it becomes a zombie until the parent collects its status using wait functions. Without this, system resources get exhausted.
g.text: When a child terminates, it remains in "zombie" state (Z) retaining process table entry
until parent calls wait(). Without wait(), zombies accumulate and exhaust system resources!

g.data-narration: The wait function family includes wait(), waitpid(), wait3(), and wait4(). Each provides different levels of control over child process synchronization.
g.text: Four main system calls for parent-child synchronization and status collection:

g.data-narration: The wait() function blocks the parent until any child terminates, returning the child's PID and storing termination status at the provided address.
g.text: ▸ Returns child PID on success, -1 on error

g.data-narration: waitpid() offers granular control with pid parameter: positive for specific child, zero for process group, negative for any child, less than negative for group ID.
g.text: Any child in same process group

g.data-narration: The options parameter uses bitwise OR to combine WNOHANG (non-blocking), WUNTRACED (stopped children), and WCONTINUED (continued children) behaviors.
WIFEXITED checks if child terminated normally via exit() or returning from main. WEXITSTATUS extracts the 8-bit exit code from the status integer.
g.text: // Returns true if normal exit (exit() or return from main)
// Returns low-order 8 bits of exit status (0-255)

g.data-narration: WIFSIGNALED returns true if child was killed by a signal. WTERMSIG provides the signal number that caused termination for further analysis.
g.text: // Returns true if child killed by a signal
// Returns signal number that killed the child (e.g., 9 for SIGKILL)

g.data-narration: WIFSTOPPED checks if child was stopped by a signal. WSTOPSIG gets the stopping signal. WIFCONTINUED checks if child resumed via SIGCONT.
wait3() and wait4() are extended versions that provide resource usage information including CPU time, page faults, and I/O operations via struct rusage.
Each process belongs to a process group identified by PGID. Child inherits parent's group. waitpid with negative pid waits for entire process groups.
g.text: → wait for any child in same process group
→ wait for any child whose PGID equals |pid|

g.data-narration: This example demonstrates basic wait() usage: fork creates child, child sleeps and exits with status 42, parent blocks on wait() and retrieves exit code.
This non-blocking example spawns 3 children, then loops with waitpid WNOHANG to collect them as they terminate without blocking the parent.
